text: The surgeon uses a Ligasure device to carefully take down adhesions resulting from a previous hysterectomy, separating the fibrous tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon encounters adhesions between the sigmoid colon and the pelvic structures, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes a large carcinoid mass with cicatrization affecting the mesentery and adjacent bowel loops, highlighting the extent of the tumor's impact on surrounding tissues.
The laparoscopic view reveals the impression of a mesenteric nodal mass located at the base of the knot, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon examines the root of the transverse mesocolon, attempting to visualize the path of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a marker tape to measure the bowel segments distal and proximal to the carcinoid mass, ensuring precise assessment of the affected bowel length.
The video shows over a meter of ileum downstream, with a significant portion of the ileum visibly entangled within the mass.
The surgeon measures approximately four meters of the upstream bowel, extending towards the duodenal jejunal flexure, with the video skipping forward to show this section.
The surgeon returns focus to the mesenteric nodal mass, preparing to lay out the bowel around the mass for further examination or manipulation.
The surgeon inserts Livac slings around the bowel to facilitate retraction and exposure during the procedure.
Elasticized bands with hooks on each end are visible, allowing adjustment of distance by selecting different holes in the band.
The surgeon grasps the peritoneum and then the peritoneum of the mesentery, preparing to manipulate these tissue layers.
The surgeon places a second Livac sling around the surgical site, likely to aid in retraction or stabilization of tissues during the procedure.
The surgeon adjusts the hook end of the Livac sling, selecting a short length to secure the sling in place.
The surgeon places a third Livac sling, visible as an elasticized band with hooks, to adjust and secure tissue during the procedure.
The mesentery is visibly laid out more clearly, showing improved exposure and organization of the tissue.
The surgeon begins by defining the transverse mesocolon, visually identifying anatomical landmarks to locate the proximal superior mesenteric artery (SMA).
The surgeon scores the peritoneum over the small bowel mesentery on the patient's left side of the mass, creating visible incisions along the tissue.
The surgeon carefully identifies the vessels supplying the affected segment of the ileum, ensuring the blood supply to the proximal small bowel remains intact.
The surgeon encounters difficulty identifying the vessels due to the presence of a very fatty peritoneum, which obscures the view of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon applies tension to the mesentery, improving its layout and visibility for further dissection.
The surgeon returns to the plane behind the transverse mesocolon mesentery, continuing the dissection in this area.
The surgeon adjusts the hooks by grabbing and repositioning them, facilitating better access and visualization of the surgical field.
The surgical field shows a layer of fatty tissue, which obscures the visibility of underlying structures and vessels.
The surgeon approaches the origin of the right colic vessels, navigating through the fatty peritoneum that obscures the visibility of these structures.
The surgeon navigates into the retroperitoneal plane, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue layers characteristic of this area.
The surgeon begins to visualize vascular anatomy within the surgical field, indicating progress in the dissection towards identifying specific blood vessels.
The surgeon navigates back to the mesentery of the small bowel on the patient's left side, adjacent to the tumor mass.
The surgeon begins dissection towards the intended line of transection, carefully separating tissue along the planned surgical path.
The surgeon continues dissection within the fatty peritoneal plane of the mesentery, revealing adipose tissue surrounding the anatomical structures.
The surgeon checks the length of the small bowel, visually assessing its position and length relative to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon works upstream of the tumor mass, using slings to stabilize the surrounding tissue and maintain clear visibility of the surgical field.
A vein becomes visible as the surgeon continues dissection towards the intended line of transection, confirming entry into the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon adjusts the hook instrument within the surgical field, likely to refine the positioning or tension on the tissue being manipulated.
The surgeon begins dissection towards the posterior leaf of the mesentery, carefully navigating the anatomical plane.
A window in the tissue plane begins to open up, revealing underlying structures as the surgeon continues dissection.
The surgeon identifies the main branch supplying the tumor mass, with the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) visible continuing down to supply the bowel proximal to the mass.

The surgeon demonstrates the limit of dissection along the blood supply, indicating that further dissection would risk devascularizing the bowel, with visible anatomical structures and vessels in the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters a sizable vessel during the dissection towards the posterior leaf of the mesentery.
The surgeon prepares to transect a large vessel using a surgical stapler, indicating the vessel's significant size within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a Covidien tan cartridge tri-stapler, 30 millimeters, to transect a large vessel near the tumor mass.

The surgeon traces the path of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) from the root of the transverse mesocolon down to a vessel visible at the bottom of the screen, highlighting the anatomical constraints that prevent further dissection without risking devascularization of the proximal small bowel.
The surgeon extends the mesenteric dissection towards the planned proximal line for bowel transection, preparing the area for further surgical intervention.

The surgeon plans the distal line of transection, with the right colic and ileocolic vessels visible in the surgical field. The ligature is prepared for dissection along a clear path, while the mass remains at a considerable distance.
The surgeon uses a combination of clips and a ligature device to secure and dissect the sizable vascular arcades visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a tan cartridge Covidien tristapler to divide the proximal end of the planned transection of the bowel.
The surgeon completes the distal transection of the bowel, marking the end of the planned transection line.
The surgeon places the resected specimen into a Covidian fifteen millimeter Endo Catch bag for removal.
The surgeon removes the 12mm port from the gel point and passes a 15mm catch bag through it, revealing a large specimen with an extended length of bowel inside the bag.
The specimen, enclosed in a catch bag, slides smoothly against the Alexis retractor. The surgeon re-examines the distal margin of the resected bowel, considering taking additional margin.

The surgeon observes a reasonable length of terminal ileum extending to the ileocecal valve. A large mesenteric window is visible, appearing disorganized. The surgeon uses a V-lock stitch to close the mesenteric window.

The surgeon operates on the thick and fatty peritoneum of the mesentery, using a needle without risk of puncturing significant vessels due to the tissue's density. The video is sped up to show the procedure at twenty times the normal speed.
The surgeon completes the closure of the mesenteric window using a suture, ensuring the mesentery is securely closed.
The surgeon uses a two-row proline suture to align the bowel, preparing for a side-to-side anastomosis in the Barcelona style.

The surgeon uses a purple, 60mm tri-stapler cartridge to align the bowel by pulling on the proline stitch, ensuring the anti-mesenteric borders are properly positioned at the crutch between the two enterotomies.
The surgeon performs a transverse firing using a surgical stapler, aligning the bowel for anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis using a tan cartridge, addressing the increased vascular supply in the mesentery. A two ovicral crutch stitch is applied at the crutch to reinforce the weak point.
The surgeon adjusts the tension of the stitch along the staple line, ensuring it is snug but not tight to prevent cutting through the tissue, while relieving tension in case of distension.
